coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of xylan-derived carbon quantum dots for tetracycline sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Pei
Zhu, Ziqi
Chen, Minzhi
Chen, Weimin
Zhou, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: A simple and direct microwave-assisted method was used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) in a single step using an ammonia solution of xylan as the precursor at a treatment temperature of 200 °C and an input power of 200 W within just 10 min. It was found that the N-CQDs synthesized by such green, economical, and fast routes possess high crystal quality, favorable photoluminescence properties, and high tolerance for salts and metal ions. More interestingly, we found that the as-prepared N-CQDs without any modification exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards tetracycline antibiotics owing to an inner-filter effect. At the optimum conditions, a novel sensing platform for sensitive detection of tetracycline in a linear range of 0.05–20 μM with a detection limit of 6.49 nM was established, suggesting a promising application for the quantification of tetracycline in biomedical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xylan
Carbon quantum dots
Tetracycline
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TC and interference experiments

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province
Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation ( BK20161524 ), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381 ), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) , and the Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX17_0839 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of N-CQDs by microwave treatment.
TEM image of the N-CQDs (a) and size distribution of the N-CQDs with Gaussian fitting (b).
Raman spectrum the N-CQDs (a). FTIR spectra of N-CQDs and xylan (b).
XPS survey (a), C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c), and O1s spectra of N-CQDs (d).
Absorption and PL emission spectra (λ ex = 360 nm) of the N-CQDs (a). PL emission spectra of the N-CQDs at increasing excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 460 nm in 20 nm increments (b).
Effect of pH on the PL intensities of the N-CQDs (a). Effect of NaCl concentration on the PL intensities of the N-CQDs (b).

Emission spectra (λex = 360 nm) of the N-CQDs in the absence (red curve) and presence (blue curve) of TC (50 μmol/L) (a). Effect of pH on the relative PL intensity of N-CQDs (b). Effect of different metal ions on the PL intensity of N-CQDs (c). Effect of different biological agents on the PL intensity of N-CQDs (d). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The dependence of PL intensity on the concentration of TC before (a) and after (b) three months. The corresponding insert shows the linear relationship between F0/F and TC concentration (0.05–20 μM).

Absorption spectrum of TC and maximum excitation spectrum of the N-CQDs (a). Fluorescence decay curves of pure N-CQDs and N-CQDs after interacting with TC (b). Fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs in the absence (c) and presence (d) of TC are fitted by two-order exponential functions, and the fitting equations are shown in the corresponding panel.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of xylan-derived carbon quantum dots for tetracycline sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and direct microwave-assisted method was used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) in a single step using an ammonia solution of xylan as the precursor at a treatment temperature of 200 °C and an input power of 200 W within just 10 min. It was found that the N-CQDs synthesized by such green, economical, and fast routes possess high crystal quality, favorable photoluminescence properties, and high tolerance for salts and metal ions. More interestingly, we found that the as-prepared N-CQDs without any modification exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards tetracycline antibiotics owing to an inner-filter effect. At the optimum conditions, a novel sensing platform for sensitive detection of tetracycline in a linear range of 0.05–20 μM with a detection limit of 6.49 nM was established, suggesting a promising application for the quantification of tetracycline in biomedical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which belong to the family of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted considerable attention owing to their superior photoluminescence (PL) [1,2], ease of functionalization [3,4], low toxicity, and biocompatibility [5,6]. These unique physical and chemical properties make CQDs excellent candidates for replacing traditional metal-based semiconductor quantum dots for application in photoelectric devices [7,8], photocatalysts [9], cell imaging [6,10], and fluorescent probes [3,11–14]. In the past few years, a number of synthesis methods have been proposed for CQDs, such as arc discharge treatment [15], electrochemical oxidation [16], laser ablation [17], nitric acid refluxing treatment [18], and ultrasonic processing [19]. Despite intensive efforts, most of the developed approaches are unsatisfactory owing to the high cost of the starting materials, complicated procedures, harsh synthesis conditions, and energy consuming equipment. Unlike the approaches mentioned above, hydrothermal treatment [20,21] is a feasible route in which a mass of low-cost organic substances serve as the carbon source for preparing water-soluble CQDs. However, the greatest disadvantage of this approach is that the processing times are, in general, very long. Thus, it is of great interest to develop a rapid and simple method for the synthesis of CQDs.

As we know, microwave irradiation, which allows for intensive, efficient, and homogeneous energy distribution, is more effective than the traditional hydrothermal treatment in accelerating the reaction rate in an aqueous phase; there are several reports on the successful application of microwave radiation to synthesize CQDs, which illustrate the feasibility and promising potential of this technique. For instance, Liu et al. reported a microwave pyrolysis route for the preparation of CQDs from glycerol in just a few minutes [22]. Chen et al. synthesized heteroatom-doped CQDs from polyols by microwave pyrolysis in a one-step reaction [23]. Xiao et al. and Gong et al. employed microwave-assisted methods to prepare chitosan-derived CQDs [24,25]. However, using a household microwave oven as a reactor to prepare CQDs (as detailed in previous reports) might be highly energy consuming because its input power is usually greater than 700 W and such equipment is limited to afford ordinary pressure condition for reaction system. Thus, a CEM Discover microwave synthesis system, which has superior and accurate control over temperature, input power, and pressure was used in the present investigation.

Thus far, many different kinds of natural biomass substrates, such as proteins [26,27], carbohydrates [6,28], and alpha hydroxy acids [29–32], have been used to synthesis CQDs. This is highly encouraging, but most food or agriculture-based biomasses are unsuitable for the large-scale synthesis of CQDs. Considering that xylan, which is a main component of hemicellulose in plant cells, is very abundant in nature, we used this biomass as a green and low-cost carbon source to synthesize CQDs using a CEM Discover microwave synthesis system with low energy consumption in an enclosed reaction environment. Additionally, an economical ammonia solution was used as the source of nitrogen as well as to disperse xylan; doping with N is an effective method to alter the electronic density of CQDs and further enhance their fluorescence properties [6,25,27]. The polymerization, carbonization, surface functionalization, and nitrogen doping processes are completed simultaneously within just a few minutes in the CEM Discover microwave synthesis system, leading to the formation of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) in a single step. More interestingly, such unmodified nanoparticles can be used as fluorescent probes to detect the presence of tetracycline (TC); such probes exhibit intrinsic properties of simplicity, rapidity, low cost, and high sensitivity.

Xylan (85%) and quinine sulfate (99.0%) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Purified water (Hangzhou, China) was used for all the experiments, NH4OH (ammonium hydroxide) was procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Tetracycline hydrochloride and other biological reagents were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH values were obtained from Haibiao Technologies Co., Ltd. All other reagents were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Xylan was purified using sodium hydroxide and all other reagents were used as received.

Xylan (0.5 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of purified water, followed by the introduction of 100 μL NH4OH (25–28 wt %). The mixture was then treated in a CEM Discover microwave system (MATTHEWS.NC, USA) at 200 W and 200 °C for 10 min. The synthesis route is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . When exposed to microwave radiation, the color of the solution changed from pale yellow to brown, indicating the formation of N-CQDs; the supernatant of the obtained reaction mixture was simply filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove the precipitate after cooling to ambient temperature. Prior to further characterization, the aqueous solution containing N-CQDs was freeze-dried. Additionally, CQDs without N-doping were synthesized in conditions similar to those described above.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were generated using a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (FEI, USA) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Raman spectra of the synthesized N-CQDs were recorded using a DXR532 Raman spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm using an argon ion laser. The infrared spectra of the synthesized CQDs were recorded on a Vertex 80V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Absorption spectra were obtained using a Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed on an AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer (Shimadzu, UK) with an ultrahigh vacuum generator. All fluorescent spectral measurements were performed on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with excitation and emission slit set at a 10-nm band pass in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz. The surface charge of the N-CQDs was measured using a ZEN3600 Zeta Plus (Malvern, United Kingdom) instrument with a He-Ne laser source. Time-resolved fluorescence decays were measured using a FLS 980 spectrometer (EI, UK) and the obtained lifetime decay curves were fitted using a double-exponential function Y(t) (eq (1)) and the average lifetime was calculated according to eq (2).
(1)
Y
( t )
=
A 1
e x p
(
− t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e x p
(
− t /
τ 2
)
(2)
τ ∗
=
(
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
)
/
(
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
)
Here, A1 and A2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved fluorescence decay lifetimes τ1 and τ2, respectively, and τ* refers to the average lifetime.

Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Φ = 54%) was chosen as the standard and the quantum yield (QY) of N-CQDs was determined at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm using a comparative method as follows.
(3)
Q x
=
Q
s t
(
G r a
d x
/ G r a
d
s t
)
(
η x 2
/
η
s t
2
)
Here, Q stands for quantum yield. Grad refers to the gradient of the plot of integrated fluorescence peak area against absorbance. The refractive index (η) of the solvent is 1.33. The subscripts x and st correspond to N-CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. In particular, the absorbance was adjusted to less than 0.05 to prevent re-absorption by both N-CQD and quinine sulfate solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC and interference experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N-CQDs were mixed with PBS buffer (0.10 M, pH = 5.0) in a 10 mL volumetric flask to create solutions of 0.3 mg/mL concentration. Subsequently, different amounts of TC were added to the N-CQD solutions. Unless otherwise stated, all the samples had an N-CQD concentration of 0.3 mg/mL and they were used for fluorescence measurements. Interference experiments were performed to confirm whether the detection system is selective; analysis was carried out based on the principle of fluorescence quenching. Specifically, various of representative metal ions, such as Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+ were added into PBS buffer (0.10 M, pH = 5.0) at a concentration of 50 μM. In addition, several biological agents, including chlorotetracycline, oxytetracycline, l-ascorbic acid, l-histidine, amoxicillin, ibuprofen, glutathione, streptomycin, penicillin G Na, melamine, glucose, lincomycin, and l-cysteine were dissolved in purified water to create solutions of 50 μM. The fluorescence response characteristics of N-CQDs in the presence of various metal ions and biological agents were investigated; all other detection conditions were the same as those used to detect TC. PL intensity measurements were carried out in triplicate and the average values were calculated.

The morphologies and dimensions of the N-CQDs were analyzed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the N-CQDs exhibit an almost spherical morphology without noticeable aggregation at room temperature; the HR-TEM image of the N-CQDs (inset of Fig. 2a) exhibits clear lattice fringes. ImageJ software was used to measure the particle size of the N-CQDs and the corresponding particle size histogram (Fig. 2b) was acquired by statistical analysis of around 120 nanoparticles. It can be observed from the histogram that the N-CQD size ranges from 4.44 nm to 13.34 nm with an average diameter of 7.89 nm. Raman spectroscopy was used to analyze the defects in the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, two predominant peaks can be observed at 1330 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, which are attributed to the D and G bands, respectively. The intensity ratio of the “disordered” D-band and crystalline G-band (I D /I G) of the N-CQDs is about 0.4, which indicates a graphitic carbon structure for the N-CQDs; this observation agrees well with the HR-TEM results. The N-CQDs synthesized in this study exhibited a high-quality graphite structure, which might be related to the reaction conditions. The high-strength microwave radiation and NH3 derived from NH4OH during microwave heating led to a high energy and pressure in the reactor, which facilitated the dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization of xylan in a hydrothermal environment. Thus, we speculate that intermediate products or related structures of xylan inclined to form the ordered structure of graphitic crystals during the formation of C spheres in the reaction condition.

To systematically investigate the chemical structure of the as-prepared N-CQDs, FTIR spectra were recorded to identify the surface groups of xylan and the as-prepared N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3b, the broad absorption band in the region of 3580–3200 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H stretching vibrations of both xylan and N-CQDs, but in the case of N-CQDs, this peak might also indicate N-H stretching vibrations. In addition, a C-H stretching vibration (2925 cm−1) and -OH in-plane bending vibration (1353 cm−1) are found in both xylan and N-CQDs. Absorption appearing at 1726 cm−1 is related to the C=O conjugated in xylan and the peak at 1640 cm−1 characterizes the absorbed water [33]. Moreover, the characteristic absorption peak at 895 cm−1 is attributed to the β-glucosidic bonds of xylan. The FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs exhibits several peaks different from those of xylan. Specifically, the broad absorption peak at 1600 cm−1 can be attributed to the overlap of the stretching vibrations of C=C and bending vibrations of N-H. Moreover, the absorption peaks corresponding to C-N/-COO– (1400 cm−1) and C-O (1068 cm−1) stretching are also observed in N-CQDs. The differences in the FTIR spectra of xylan and N-CQDs demonstrate that there is a remarkable chemical transformation when xylan is subjected to intensive microwave treatment.

To further investigate the chemical states of the elements, XPS analysis of the N-CQDs was carried out (Fig. 4 a). The full-range spectrum shows mainly three bonding energy peaks of C1s at 284.8 eV, N1s at 399.5 eV, and O1s at 532.0 eV. Additionally, a small peak at 495.0 eV confirms the existence of Na, which might have been derived from the residues of NaOH in the refinement process of xylan. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 4b) exhibits four main peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 286.3, and 288.6 eV, which are attributed to the bonding energies of C-C/C=C, C-N, C-O, and C=O, respectively [34]. The N1s spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 4c) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.4 and 400.8 eV, corresponding to the pyrrolic N and amine groups, respectively [29,35]. It can be seen that pyrrolic N formed by the dehydrolysis reaction between neighboring carboxyl and amine groups is the main doping component of N in the as-prepared N-CQDs. Pyrrolic N, which acts as an electron donor can increase the electronic cloud density on the surfaces of the CQDs, which in turn enhances the QY of the CQDs [36]. In the O1S spectrum (Fig. 4d), the peaks at 532.8 and 531.4 eV are assigned to the binding energies of C-OH/C-O-C and C=O, respectively [35]. Both XPS and FTIR results confirm the presence of oxygen-containing groups and nitrogen-containing groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs and these hydrophilic functional groups endow the N-CQDs with excellent water solubility.

The optical performance of the N-CQDs was characterized by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and PL measurements. The fluorescence QYs of the N-CQDs and xylan-derived N-CQDs without N doping were calculated using equation (3) to be ∼4.0% and 2.0%, respectively; this result further illustrates that doping with N enhances fluorescence QY. Fig. 5 a depicts the absorption spectrum and maximum emission of the as-prepared N-CQDs. The absorption peak at 270 nm reflects the aromatic π system in N-CQDs [23]. The maximum emission spectrum with a peak position at 442 nm obtained at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm suggests a blue emission behavior for the synthesized N-CQDs; the full width at half maximum is only 108 nm, further confirming the narrow size distribution of the as-prepared N-CQDs. Additionally, increasing excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 460 nm were applied to verify the PL results and the obtained emission spectra are shown in Fig. 5b. Preeminently, the emission peaks gradually shifted from 412 nm to 510 nm with an increasing excitation wavelength; the corresponding fluorescent intensity increased and reached a maximum value at 360 nm excitation and then decreased with further increase in the excitation wavelength, which demonstrates the typical excitation wavelength-dependent behavior of the CQDs. Such luminescence properties, which can lead to multicolor fluorescence, may be related to the optical selection of N-CQDs with varying sizes (quantum confinement effect) and emission traps on the N-CQD surface via radiative recombination induced by electron-hole pairs [23,37].

To further investigate the fluorescence stability of the as-prepared N-CQDs, they were dissolved in PBS buffers at different pH values and the maximum PL intensities (λ ex = 360 nm) of N-CQDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the PL intensities increased initially with an increase in the pH and reached the maximum value at pH = 5; beyond this point, the fluorescence of the N-CQDs was quenched significantly. The variation in the PL intensities with respect to pH can be attributed to the effect of protonation and deprotonation. Specifically, the large number of -COOH groups on the surface of N-CQDs (identified by FTIR) might reach ionization equilibrium at pH = 5; such equal numbers of electrons and holes on the surfaces of the N-CQDs result in an optimal passivation and maximum PL intensity [18]. The effect of saline ions on the PL stability of N-CQDs was also investigated (Fig. 6b) and it could be noticed that there is no significant alteration in the PL intensity with an increase in the NaCl concentration, indicating that the N-CQDs have high salt tolerance. Such a feature is beneficial when N-CQDs are applied in biomedical devices.

To further analyze the potential applications of the as-prepared N-CQDs, we evaluated these nanoparticles as fluorescent probes. It is attractive that the fluorescence of N-CQDs without any further chemical modification can be quenched by TC. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the PL intensity decreased sharply once 50 μM TC was injected into N-CQDs solution (0.3 mg/mL), which suggests that the fluorescence of N-CQDs is sensitive to TC. Moreover, as previously described, pH has a significant influence on the PL intensity; thus, it is necessary to analyze quenching efficiency as a function of pH. As shown in Fig. 7b, the PL intensities of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of TC are denoted by F 0 and F, respectively; the quenching efficiency (F 0/F) was found to be constant in the pH range of 3–11. Such pH-independent behavior of the quenching efficiency can be attributed to the influence of different hydrion concentrations on the as-prepared N-CQDs rather than on TC. It can be inferred from these results that the detection system is not limited by different acid-base environments, which is highly advantageous for the application of N-CQDs in biomedical devices. As the maximum PL intensity of the as-prepared N-CQDs is achieved at pH = 5, the PBS buffer with a pH value of 5 was chosen as the solvent for the detection system in all the subsequent experiments. To further confirm whether the effect of TC on N-CQDs is selective, interference experiments were carried out at pH = 5. Various cations, such as Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+, each at a concentration of 50 μM, were reacted with the N-CQDs. Surprisingly, fluorescence quenching was not induced by any of the tested metal ions (Fig. 7c); this observation is drastically different from the results reported in other studies [3,25,38–41]. Therefore, this difference demonstrates that the N-CQDs exhibit higher tolerance towards metal ions. In addition, several biological agents, including tetracycline, chlorotetracycline, oxytetracycline, l-ascorbic acid, l-histidine, amoxicillin, ibuprofen, glutathione, streptomycin, penicillin G Na, melamine, glucose, lincomycin, and l-cysteine, each at a concentration of 50 μM, were added to the N-CQDs solutions, whose fluorescence intensities were subsequently measured. Tetracycline-based antibiotics, such as tetracycline, chlorotetracycline, and oxytetracycline, exerted a strong effect on the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs compared to other biological molecules (Fig. 7d). Based on these results, it can be deduced that the as-prepared N-CQDs without any further chemical modification might be promising fluorescent probes to detect TC. Hence, a detection system was developed at the optimum conditions. Considering that mild conditions are desired in practical applications, the incubation temperature was maintained at 30 °C by water-bath heating; the incubation time was also taken into account and the final results revealed that the PL intensities constantly decreased until the reaction time reached 30 min.

At the established optimum condition, the maximum PL intensity of N-CQDs (λ ex = 360 nm) decreased gradually when the concentration of TC increased from 0 to 200 μM, and the relationship between PL intensity and TC concentration can be fit well with an exponential attenuation model (Fig. 8 a). Moreover, the analytical performance of N-CQDs illustrated that there exists a nice linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F 0/F) and TC concentration in the range of 0.05 μM–20 μM (see the inset of Fig. 8a), and the relationship can be described according to the following equation:
(4)
F 0
/ F = 0.0173
C
T C
+ 1.012 ,
R 2
= 0.998
where C TC is the concentration of TC in the range of 0.05–20 μM. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated according to LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the slope of the regression equation and S is the standard deviation of the blank solution (n = 11). In this case, the LOD for TC is about 6.49 nM. To further investigate the stability of the proposed method for TC detection, a cycles of experiment was performed to reexamine the sensitivity of the N-CQDs-based sensors. The as-prepared N-CQDs which have been stored for about three months by a simple ultrasound treatment was used to detect TC in the same condition as the first detection experiment. As depicted in Fig. 8b, the PL intensities also showed an exponential attenuation with the increasing of TC concentration, and a good linear correlation was observed between the quenching efficiency (F 0/F) and the TC concentration of 0.05–20 μM, the fitted curve equation is F 0/F = 0.0186C TC+1.013. The results obtained from retest illustrated that the slope value for TC determination without significant change. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for five repeated determination of 5 μM TC was 2.78%. Therefore, the developed sensing platform was satisfactory and suitable for the determination of TC.

Several techniques, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [42], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [43], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [44], and LC with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [45], have been used for the detection of TC. However, most of these detection techniques are costly, labor intensive, time consuming, and lack specificity. In striking contrast to these traditional methods, the as-prepared N-CQDs without any further modification offer a promising fluorescent assay for tetracycline antibiotics.

To further understand the working principle of the detection system, the optical properties of TC and the as-prepared N-CQDs were studied. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, the absorption spectrum of TC exhibited a broad peak at 375 nm overlapped by the maximum excitation spectrum of the as-prepared N-CQDs, indicating that TC exerted an inner filter effect on the N-CQDs. Specifically, energy transfer occurred between N-CQDs and TC due to the absorption of TC at a wavelength of 375 nm, while the N-CQDs emitted photons. The inner filter effect may also be related to the absorption of light by TC. This leads to a decrease in the number of photons available to work on N-CQDs; thus, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs decreased. Additionally, the fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of TC were measured and compared to obtain further information on the interaction between them (Fig. 9b). According to eq. (1) and eq. (2), the calculated average lifetimes of the N-CQDs in the absence and presence of TC are 1.35 and 1.08 ns, respectively (Fig. 9c and Fig. 9d). The almost constant fluorescence lifetime values indicate that the interaction between TC and N-CQDs can be attributed to a static quenching effect [46]. Accordingly, the reduced PL intensity may be attributed to the formation of ground-state non-fluorescent complexes when N-CQDs coexist with TC. Zeta potential is a parameter which can confirm whether electrostatic adhesion accelerated the interaction between the as-prepared N-CQDs and TC; the zeta potential values of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of TC were −14.8 eV and −13.5 eV, respectively. The relatively small difference indicates a very small effect of electrostatic adhesion. Thus, the working mechanism of the detection system can be attributed to an inner filter effect, which can in turn be attributed to the formation of a complex between TC and the as-prepared N-CQDs.

In summary, we used a CEM Discover microwave synthesis system with low energy consumption and an enclosed reaction environment to synthesize fluorescent N-CQDs from dilute ammonia solutions of xylan within 10 min. These nanoparticles could not only be obtained easily and rapidly from renewable and low-cost xylan, but they also exhibited high crystal quality, favorable PL properties, and high tolerance for salts and metal ions. Interestingly, the as-prepared N-CQDs were found to be promising fluorescent probes to detect TC. A good linear relationship exists between the quenching efficiency and TC concentration in the range of 0.05–20 μM; the detection limit was 6.49 nM. Furthermore, the working mechanism of the detection system was analyzed. It is attributed to an inner-filter effect owing to the formation of a complex between TC and the N-CQDs. The environmentally friendly and cost-effective N-CQDs developed in this study have the potential for a wide range of applications in the biomedical field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation (BK20161524), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_0839).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and efficient synthesis of strongly green fluorescent carbon dots with upconversion property for direct cell imaging
